title: Agentic HR Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform for HR agents — candidate screening and recruiting, employee helpdesk and policy Q&A, and onboarding — where the highest-stakes action is a hiring decision, which is legally consequential and hard to reverse. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds two vertical-specific things: an effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged policy-KB grounding corpus with PII-scoped retrieval, and a hiring review gate where agents only recommend and humans decide every adverse outcome, with adverse-impact (four-fifths-rule) monitoring built in. Compliance is first-class: NYC Local Law 144 requires an annual bias audit, public posting of the results, and candidate notice for automated employment decision tools (enforced since July 5, 2023); the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) classifies Annex III employment/recruitment uses as high-risk with staged application dates under Article 113.

nodes.label: Recruiter / Hiring Mgr
nodes.description: Owns hiring decisions; reviews and decides every adverse outcome.

nodes.label: Employee / Candidate
nodes.description: Asks HR questions or applies; their PII must stay scoped to them.

nodes.label: HR Request
nodes.description: A screening task, an onboarding trigger, or an employee help query.

nodes.label: HR API
nodes.description: Authenticates the request, resolves the requester's identity scope, routes.

nodes.label: HR Orchestrator
nodes.description: Routes to the right flow: screening, helpdesk, or onboarding; deterministic where decisions matter.

nodes.label: Screening Agent
nodes.description: LLM node that scores candidates on job-related skills only — never on prohibited signals.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: Policy KB
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged HR policy and benefits docs.

nodes.label: ATS / HRIS
nodes.description: Workday/SuccessFactors/ADP: candidate and employee records, the action surface.

nodes.label: Adverse-Impact Monitor
nodes.description: Instruments selection rates by group and computes the four-fifths rule for bias auditing.

nodes.label: Hiring Review Gate
nodes.description: Routes adverse outcomes to a human; agents recommend, humans decide.

nodes.label: PII-Scoped Retrieval
nodes.description: Enforces identity-scoped retrieval: an employee's query can only reach their own record.

nodes.label: Recommendation Store
nodes.description: Pending screening recommendations awaiting a human hiring decision.

nodes.label: Workflow Engine
nodes.description: Durable engine running onboarding/offboarding as idempotent HRIS tasks with retry, timeout, and reconciliation.

nodes.label: HRIS Task Queue
nodes.description: Levels bursty screening and throttled HRIS writes; idempotent, keyed tasks survive partial failure and duplicate callbacks.

nodes.label: Workflow State
nodes.description: Workflow runs, per-task state, and reconciliation events — the durable record of long-running provisioning.

nodes.label: Policy & Privacy
nodes.description: Prohibited-signal exclusion, data residency, tenant isolation, untrusted-text screening.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access; binds hiring decisions to a human principal; enforces PII scope.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only record: features used, recommendations, human decisions, adverse-impact metrics.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, selection-rate dashboards, deflection/accuracy and fairness eval.

functional: Screen candidates on job-related skills and surface recommendations.
Answer employee policy/benefits questions grounded in current, jurisdiction-correct policy.
Orchestrate onboarding/offboarding actions against the HRIS.
Route every adverse hiring outcome to a human decision-maker.
Monitor selection rates for adverse impact (four-fifths rule).
nonFunctional: Fairness: score on job-related features only; exclude prohibited signals and proxies; no emotion/biometric inference.
Humans decide hiring; agents only recommend — adverse decisions are never automated.
PII privacy: identity-scoped retrieval (an employee can't reach another's record); data residency.
Grounding in effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged policy (not stale or wrong-jurisdiction answers).
Compliance and auditability: NYC LL144 bias audits, EU AI Act high-risk obligations; logged decisions.

capacity.label: Candidate screenings
capacity.value: ~5k/day steady, ~25k/day burst (~5x) after a posting wave
capacity.note: Recruiting is bursty: a new req or campus posting can 5x daily applications. The work queue levels the burst; the LLM is ~1 scoring call/candidate, not the hot path.

capacity.label: Helpdesk / policy QPS
capacity.value: ~20 QPS steady, ~150 QPS at open enrollment (~7x)
capacity.note: Benefits/leave questions spike during open enrollment and major policy changes. Cache hot policy answers per (jurisdiction, effective-date) to absorb the spike.

capacity.label: Onboarding/offboarding
capacity.note: Provisioning is multi-step and long-running. HRIS/ATS APIs throttle (~2–10 writes/sec/tenant), so writes go through an idempotent workflow queue, not synchronous fan-out.

capacity.label: Human review load
capacity.value: ~30% of screenings reach a human (~1.5k/day), ~24h decision SLA
capacity.note: At ~50 decisions/reviewer/day that is ~30 reviewers; adverse-impact flags and appeals add escalation load on top.

capacity.label: Policy corpus / vector index
capacity.value: ~50k chunks across jurisdictions/versions, daily-to-weekly update cadence
capacity.note: Small enough for a single managed vector index with metadata filters; superseded versions are retained for audit, not deleted.

capacity.label: Audit retention
capacity.value: recommendations/decisions kept ~3–7 yrs; adverse-impact snapshots per audit cycle
capacity.note: Compliance (LL144 annual bias audit, EU AI Act records) drives retention and legal hold, not storage cost; the audit stream is append-only.

capacity.label: Adverse-impact batch
capacity.value: nightly aggregation; min cohort ~30–50 before a four-fifths flag is trusted
capacity.note: Selection rates are computed on a rolling window; small cohorts are reported with a low-confidence caveat rather than a hard flag.

capacity.label: Latency / SLA targets
capacity.value: policy answer p95 ~2–3s; review-queue age <24h; HRIS op completion minutes–hours; adverse-impact freshness ~24h
capacity.note: The human review queue, not the LLM, is the binding SLA: a recommendation must reach a decision within ~24h, and the queue is sized to reviewer throughput. HRIS operations are eventually consistent (the workflow may sit in 'reconciling'); adverse-impact metrics are at most a day stale.

api.description: Screen a candidate on skills; returns a recommendation for human review — never a decision. Idempotent on idempotencyKey so a retried submit does not create a duplicate screening; the tenant is derived from the auth token, not the body.

A human recruiter records the hiring decision; the agent never decides an adverse outcome. The deciding principal is taken from the auth token (not the body), the call is idempotent on decisionIdempotencyKey, and the response returns the audit correlation id so the decision is traceable end-to-end.
Fetch selection-rate-by-group metrics and four-fifths-rule flags for the bias audit.
Answer an employee policy/benefits question, grounded in the policy in force for their jurisdiction and date, with citations. Retrieval is scoped to the authenticated identity.
api.request: { "question": "..." }  (identity & jurisdiction resolved from the auth token, not the body)

api.description: Start an onboarding or offboarding workflow; provisioning/deprovisioning runs as idempotent HRIS operations with reconciliation. Returns a workflow handle to poll.
Fetch onboarding/offboarding workflow state, per-task status, and any reconciliation exceptions awaiting attention.
List the pending hiring-review queue for a recruiter: recommendations awaiting a human decision, with feature evidence and any adverse-impact flag, plus assignment and SLA state.
Escalate a stuck or conflicted review (SLA breach, reviewer recusal, adverse-impact flag) to a second reviewer or supervisor; the screening moves to the escalated state with a reason.

Open an HR case when a policy answer abstains or the question crosses identity scope (e.g. requires manager-only data): the LLM answer escalates to a human owner instead of guessing. Created from a policy-answer escalation rather than returned inline.
Cancel or compensate a running onboarding/offboarding workflow; reversible tasks are compensated (rolled back) and irreversible ones are flagged for manual attention.

dataModel.name: screenings
dataModel.note: A candidate screening recommendation and its review-queue state, awaiting a human decision.

dataModel.name: review_assignments
dataModel.note: Assignment and queue lifecycle of a screening to a reviewer, with SLA and escalation history.

dataModel.name: job_rubrics
dataModel.note: Versioned, job-related scoring rubric and the feature allowlist the agent may use.

dataModel.name: feature_evidence
dataModel.note: Which evidence supported each scored feature — provenance for review and audit.

dataModel.name: review_decisions
dataModel.note: Human hiring decision events on a screening recommendation — idempotent, bound to a human principal, with candidate-notice and appeal artifacts.

dataModel.name: protected_attribute_audit_data
dataModel.note: Protected-attribute data kept ONLY for fairness auditing, never exposed to scoring features. Lives in a separate, access-controlled store with its own retention.

dataModel.name: selection_metrics
dataModel.note: Adverse-impact tracking by group, computed on a rolling cohort window.

dataModel.name: policy_documents
dataModel.note: Effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged HR policy/benefits documents and their lifecycle.

fields.name: residency_region
fields.type: varchar (where the doc may be stored/served)

dataModel.name: policy_chunks
dataModel.note: Retrievable chunks with embeddings; carry tenant/residency + citation + version metadata for grounding and filtering.

dataModel.name: workflow_runs
dataModel.note: Parent record for a durable onboarding/offboarding workflow — the long-running unit of work.

dataModel.name: workflow_tasks
dataModel.note: Per-task state inside a workflow run — one row per HRIS operation, with attempt history.

dataModel.name: hris_operations
dataModel.note: Idempotent onboarding/offboarding actions against the HRIS/ATS, with reconciliation and full provider response/error history.

dataModel.name: workflow_reconciliation_events
dataModel.note: Provider callbacks, timeouts, and manual-HRIS-edit detections reconciled against expected task state.

dataModel.name: identity_scopes
dataModel.note: Resolved access scope per principal: tenant, role, region/residency, delegated act.

dataModel.name: audit_runs
dataModel.note: Dated compliance evidence packets (e.g. LL144 annual bias audit, EU AI Act records) with generated artifact location and publication approval.

dataModel.name: audit_record
dataModel.note: Append-only event stream: features, recommendations, decisions, answers, provisioning.

patterns.name: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
patterns.what: Screening is an authored flow (score -> monitor -> human decide), not an autonomous loop that could auto-reject.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A human decides every adverse hiring outcome; agents only recommend.
patterns.whenToUse: Any adverse or consequential employment decision.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: Agents recommend freely but a human-decision gate and fairness monitor fence outcomes.
patterns.whenToUse: Recommendation autonomy with a human-decision boundary.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground answers in effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged policy, not model memory.
patterns.whenToUse: Policy/benefits Q&A that must be current and correct.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Identity-scoped access binds hiring decisions to a human and PII to its owner.
patterns.whenToUse: Accessing HRIS records and recording decisions.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat resumes and free-text inputs as untrusted; partition from HRIS authority.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate policy-answer accuracy and deflection, and audit screening for bias.

patterns.name: Durable workflow with idempotency + reconciliation
patterns.what: Long-running onboarding/offboarding runs as a state machine of idempotent HRIS tasks that survive throttling, timeouts, duplicate callbacks, and manual HRIS edits.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-step, partially-failing, rate-limited writes to an external system of record.

patterns.name: Queue-based load leveling; admission control
patterns.what: Level bursty screening and throttled HRIS-write load behind a durable task queue.
patterns.whenToUse: Bursty recruiting, open-enrollment helpdesk, or rate-limited HRIS provisioning.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Screens and Rejects (the baseline)
description: Start with the demo: an agent reads resumes, scores candidates, and auto-rejects the low scores — and answers any employee's question against whatever data it can reach.

In HR this is legally hazardous. Auto-rejection is an automated adverse decision; the model may lean on proxies for protected traits, creating disparate impact with no audit; there's no fairness monitoring; and unscoped retrieval can expose one employee's PII to another.
The rest of the interview adds grounding, PII scoping, a human-decision gate, and adverse-impact monitoring.

It also leaves capacity implicit for now: screening is bursty (a posting wave can 5x daily applications) and HRIS provisioning is rate-limited, so this synchronous baseline must later move behind a durable task queue (Step 5) rather than fanning out calls inline.

Keep one distinction sharp for later: a *recommendation* is the agent's scored output; a *decision* is a human committing to an outcome. And a *meaningful* human review (sees feature evidence, records a rationale, can override) is not the same as a rubber-stamp approval click.

view.caption: An agent auto-scores and rejects candidates — the legally hazardous baseline.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is auto-rejecting candidates riskier than auto-publishing a marketing asset?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
whyNow: The naive auto-reject makes the fairness and human-decision requirements concrete.

recap.after: An agent can screen and answer HR questions.
recap.newRisk: Automated adverse decisions, possible disparate impact, no fairness audit, and PII leakage.

traps.trap: Letting the agent auto-reject low-scoring candidates.
traps.why: It's an automated adverse decision that can encode disparate impact and violate hiring law.
traps.instead: Have the agent recommend on job-related skills; a human decides, with adverse-impact monitoring.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Effective-Dated, Jurisdiction-Tagged Policy
description: For the service-delivery side, the grounding corpus is HR policy and benefits documents — but they are versioned: each is effective-dated and jurisdiction-tagged, so the agent answers from the policy in force for that employee's location and date, not a stale or wrong-jurisdiction version.

Treat the corpus as having a lifecycle, not as a static index: ingestion, approval, an effective date, expiry/supersession when a newer version takes over, jurisdiction tags, citation metadata, and cache/index invalidation when a version flips. Policy currency is the whole point — an answer must cite the version it came from.
Screening is grounded in the job's required skills. Resumes and free-text queries are treated as untrusted input.

view.caption: Answers are grounded in effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged policy — the version in force for that employee.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why isn't one policy document enough to answer an employee's benefits question?

options.name: Metadata-filtered shared index (recommended)
pros: One vector index; jurisdiction + effective-date are filter predicates on the query.
Cheapest to operate; new versions are inserts, supersession flips a date.
Cross-jurisdiction questions still answerable with explicit filters.
cons: Filter correctness is now load-bearing — a wrong filter returns a wrong-jurisdiction rule.
Tenant isolation must be enforced in the filter, not by physical separation.

options.name: Per-tenant / per-jurisdiction segmented indexes
pros: Strong physical isolation; a query can't accidentally cross a boundary.
Smaller indexes per segment; easy to reason about residency.
cons: Index sprawl and fan-out for multi-jurisdiction questions.
More operational surface to keep versions in sync.

options.name: Single global corpus, no segmentation
pros: Simplest possible to build.
Fine for a single-jurisdiction, single-tenant deployment.
cons: Can return a stale or wrong-jurisdiction policy — the core failure this step exists to prevent.
No residency or tenant isolation.

concepts.definition: Policy docs versioned by when they apply and where, so retrieval returns the rule in force for the asker.
concepts.whyItMatters: A stale or wrong-jurisdiction answer is wrong and can mislead an employee on benefits or leave.
concepts.example: A California employee asking about leave gets the current CA policy, not last year's or another state's.

whyNow: Correct grounding is the basis for trustworthy answers and defensible screening.

recap.before: Answers came from model memory or a single doc.
recap.after: Answers are grounded in the right policy version and jurisdiction.
recap.newRisk: Retrieval must not cross from one employee's data into another's.

steps.title: 3. PII-Scoped Retrieval & Identity
description: Employee data is sensitive and siloed. PII-scoped retrieval enforces that a query can only reach the requester's own record — an employee asking 'explain my pay' cannot retrieve a colleague's. The identity broker binds the scope; data residency is respected.
Resumes and inputs are partitioned from HRIS write authority so an injected instruction can't exfiltrate or alter records.

view.caption: Identity-scoped retrieval ensures a query reaches only the requester's own record; inputs are partitioned from HRIS authority.

steps.decisionPrompt: What stops an employee's helpdesk query from retrieving another employee's record?

options.name: Token-scoped service boundary (recommended)
pros: The retrieval/HRIS call carries a scoped, delegated token bound to the principal — enforcement is at the data boundary, not in the prompt.
An injected instruction can't widen scope; the token simply can't fetch another record.
Same mechanism enforces tenant and residency.
cons: Requires a real identity/token broker and per-call scope resolution.
More moving parts than a prompt rule.

options.name: Retrieval-filter enforcement
pros: Row-level filter bound to the authenticated id keeps results scoped without the agent's cooperation.
Cheaper than full token exchange.
cons: Downstream HRIS writes still need their own authority check.
A filter bug is a silent cross-record leak.

options.name: Prompt-level instruction only
pros: Trivial to add.
cons: Not an enforcement boundary — a prompt-injected query can ask for another record and get it.
Unacceptable for PII; included only to be rejected.

patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: Retrieval constrained by the requester's identity so it can only return their own records.
concepts.whyItMatters: HR holds the most sensitive employee data; cross-record access is a privacy breach.
concepts.example: The retrieval filter is bound to the authenticated employee id, not the query text.

whyNow: Privacy scoping must be enforced before any sensitive answer or action.

messages.label: scoped, delegated token (act claim, row filter)

recap.after: Each query is scoped to the requester's own data.
recap.newRisk: Screening recommendations still must not become automated adverse decisions.

traps.trap: Enforcing PII scope with a prompt instruction ('only read the asker's record').
traps.why: A prompt-injected query can ask for someone else's record and the model may comply — it's not an enforcement boundary.
traps.instead: Bind the scope to the authenticated identity at the retrieval/HRIS boundary via a scoped, delegated token or a row-level filter.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Humans Decide, Adverse-Impact Monitored
description: This is the crux. The agent scores candidates on job-related skills only and produces a recommendation; a human decides every adverse outcome — agents recommend, humans decide, and nothing auto-rejects. Identity binds the decision to a human principal.

Operationally the gate is a queue, not a callback: a pending recommendation carries the versioned job rubric, the feature evidence that supported it, an assignment, and an SLA. A reviewer must record a rationale, may override the recommendation, and may recuse on a conflict of interest. A breached SLA, a recusal, or an adverse-impact flag escalates the item to a second reviewer; an adverse decision emits the AEDT candidate notice and can be appealed. The decision is recorded idempotently against the deciding human principal, and the override, rationale, notice, and appeal are themselves audited.

In parallel, an adverse-impact monitor instruments selection rates by group and computes the four-fifths rule, flagging disparate impact for the bias audit. The protected-attribute data used for that audit lives in a separate, access-controlled store and is never a feature the scoring agent can see. Selection rates are computed over a rolling cohort window; small cohorts are reported with a low-confidence caveat rather than a hard flag. Prohibited signals and proxies are excluded from the feature allowlist the agent may use.

view.caption: Agents recommend on skills; a human decides every adverse outcome; selection rates are monitored for adverse impact.

steps.decisionPrompt: Where does the human sit in the loop, and what does the four-fifths rule flag?

options.name: Pre-decision review queue (recommended)
pros: Nothing adverse happens until a human decides — the recommendation sits in a queue with feature evidence.
Reviewer identity, rationale, and recusal are captured before any HRIS write.
Cleanest audit story for LL144 / EU AI Act.
cons: Adds latency and reviewer staffing load for every adverse outcome.
Queue can back up; needs SLA and escalation.

options.name: Thresholded escalation
pros: Only borderline or flagged recommendations go to a human; clear-advance cases flow faster.
Concentrates reviewer time where it matters.
cons: An adverse outcome below the threshold could auto-apply — must guarantee every *adverse* outcome still routes to a human.
Thresholds are themselves a fairness-sensitive policy.

options.name: Post-hoc review (audit after the fact)
pros: No latency added to the candidate path.
cons: The adverse decision already happened — this is auditing, not a human deciding.
Fails the 'humans decide every adverse outcome' requirement; included to be rejected.

concepts.definition: If a group's selection rate is below 80% of the highest group's, that signals potential disparate impact warranting review.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the standard bias-audit measure (and underlies NYC LL144); building it in makes fairness measurable.
concepts.example: If group A is selected at 50% and group B at 30%, the ratio 0.6 < 0.8 is flagged.

concepts.definition: Demographic data used to measure adverse impact is stored apart from, and never joined into, the features the scoring model can read.
concepts.whyItMatters: You need the data to audit fairness, but if it can reach scoring it becomes a direct discrimination vector.
concepts.example: selection_metrics reads protected_attribute_audit_data; the screening agent only sees the job rubric's feature allowlist.

whyNow: The human-decision gate plus monitoring is what makes agentic screening lawful and fair.

recap.before: The agent could decide outcomes and bias went unmeasured.
recap.after: Humans decide; selection rates are monitored for adverse impact.
recap.newRisk: Compliance regimes and quality must be operationalized and audited.

traps.trap: Treating fairness as 'the model is unbiased'.
traps.why: Models learn proxies; without measurement, disparate impact is invisible.
traps.instead: Exclude prohibited signals and instrument selection rates with the four-fifths rule.

steps.title: 5. Service Delivery, Compliance & Evaluation
description: Round out the platform. The helpdesk deflects routine questions (grounded, PII-scoped) and escalates the rest; onboarding/offboarding orchestrates HRIS actions as a durable, idempotent workflow rather than a synchronous chain of calls.

Compliance is first-class and source-backed: NYC LL144 requires an annual bias audit, public posting of results, and candidate notice for AEDTs (enforced since July 5, 2023); the EU AI Act (Reg. 2024/1689) treats Annex III hiring uses as high-risk with staged Article 113 dates. Produce dated evidence packets (audit runs), not just generic logs. Every recommendation, decision, policy answer, and provisioning action is audited.
Evaluate both quality (deflection and answer accuracy) and fairness (selection-rate dashboards), so an efficient helpdesk never hides a wrong policy answer or a biased screen.

view.caption: Onboarding/offboarding runs as a durable workflow of idempotent HRIS tasks with reconciliation; helpdesk and compliance (LL144, EU AI Act) are evaluated and audited.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which compliance obligations attach to automated hiring tools, and how do you evidence them?

options.name: Workflow queue with idempotency + reconciliation (recommended)
pros: HRIS writes are idempotent (keyed) and survive throttling, partial failure, and duplicate webhooks.
Long-running provisioning is a state machine with reconciliation, not a synchronous call that can half-complete.
Reversible vs irreversible actions are tracked explicitly.
cons: More infrastructure than a direct call.
Eventual consistency: the workflow can be in 'reconciling' for a while.

options.name: Synchronous HRIS writes
pros: Simple; caller learns the result immediately.
cons: HRIS rate limits and timeouts cause partial provisioning and duplicate effects.
No clean recovery when a write times out after it actually succeeded.

concepts.definition: Regimes like NYC LL144 require periodic bias audits and candidate notice for automated employment decision tools.
concepts.whyItMatters: Automated hiring is regulated; the platform must produce audit evidence, not just function.
concepts.example: Publish a bias-audit summary and notify candidates that an automated tool is used.

whyNow: Compliance and evaluation are what let the platform operate lawfully at scale.

deepDives.title: What the market retreated from
points: Emotion/affect and biometric inference are avoided (and prohibited in some regimes).
Skills-based scoring with monitored selection rates is the defensible approach.
Keep raw inputs and model traces access-controlled and residency-aware.

recap.before: Service and compliance were ad hoc.
recap.after: Helpdesk and onboarding run under compliance; quality and fairness are evaluated.
recap.newRisk: None new — the platform is now complete: grounded answers, scoped PII, a human gate, monitored fairness, and durable provisioning, all audited.

traps.trap: Treating onboarding as a synchronous sequence of HRIS calls.
traps.why: HRIS/ATS APIs throttle, time out, and deliver duplicate webhooks, so a synchronous chain leaves half-provisioned employees and double effects on retry.
traps.instead: Run provisioning as a durable workflow of idempotent operations with reconciliation, tracking reversible vs irreversible actions.

traps.why: Compliance regimes want dated, scoped evidence packets (bias audit for a period, candidate notices issued), not a searchable firehose.
traps.instead: Produce audit runs: a dated metrics snapshot per regime with retention and legal hold.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic HR Platform
finalDesign.description: Requests enter through the HR API, which resolves the requester's identity scope. The orchestrator routes to screening, helpdesk, or onboarding. Screening agents score candidates on job-related skills only (prohibited signals excluded) and write recommendations; an adverse-impact monitor instruments selection rates and computes the four-fifths rule. A human decides every adverse hiring outcome — agents recommend, humans decide — with the decision bound to a human principal. Helpdesk answers are grounded in effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged policy and retrieved under PII scope so a query reaches only the requester's own record; onboarding/offboarding runs as idempotent HRIS operations through a durable workflow with reconciliation, not synchronous fan-out. The platform's artifacts are explicit: a policy answer with citations, a screening recommendation with feature evidence against a versioned job rubric, a human decision event with rationale, an HRIS operation with an idempotency key, an adverse-impact metric snapshot, and a dated audit run for compliance. Compliance (NYC LL144, EU AI Act high-risk) and an append-only audit are first-class, and quality plus fairness are evaluated. Policy retrieval, identity/PII scoping, guardrails, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; the gate protects a legally consequential hiring decision and fairness.

functional.how: Skills-only scoring against a versioned job rubric, producing a recommendation with feature evidence — never a decision.
Grounded in effective-dated, jurisdiction-tagged policy with citations; abstains to an HR case when no in-force policy is found.
A durable workflow of idempotent HRIS operations with reconciliation, not synchronous fan-out, run under scoped identity.
Recommendations sit in an assigned, SLA-tracked review queue; a human principal records an idempotent decision with rationale and recusal.
Nightly selection-rate aggregation and the four-fifths rule, with low-confidence flags for small cohorts and protected attributes kept apart from scoring.

nonFunctional.how: Prohibited signals excluded from the feature allowlist; selection rates monitored with the four-fifths rule and low-confidence small-cohort flags.
Identity-scoped, token/PDP-enforced retrieval to the requester's own record, with cross-scope requests refused and logged; residency carried on the records.
NYC LL144 and EU AI Act obligations evidenced as dated audit runs with artifact storage, publication approval, retention, and legal hold.
Append-only audit of recommendations, human decisions, policy answers with citations, and HRIS provisioning, correlated end-to-end.

tradeoff: The corpus is small (~50k chunks) but metadata-heavy; the real work is jurisdiction + effective-date filtering, so pick a backend with first-class metadata filters.
A managed index removes embedding-pipeline and scaling ops but you still own version/supersession logic.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Strong metadata filtering makes per-jurisdiction physical index sprawl unnecessary.

tradeoff: Provisioning is long-running with retries, idempotency, and reconciliation — a durable workflow engine, not ad-hoc cron, is the right primitive.
Self-hosted Temporal gives full control of retries/compensation; a managed engine cuts operational burden but constrains execution semantics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable engine with idempotent activities removes hand-rolled retry/dedup state for HRIS writes.

tradeoff: Scoped, delegated tokens (act claim) plus a PDP enforce PII scope at the data boundary rather than in the prompt.
OPA/SpiceDB give fine-grained, testable authz; cloud IdPs integrate faster but couple you to one provider's token model.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Token-scoped boundaries make prompt-level 'don't read other records' instructions irrelevant as a security control.

tradeoff: Compliance needs dated, tamper-evident evidence packets with retention and legal hold, not just searchable logs.
WORM/object-lock storage gives defensible immutability; a plain log is cheaper but weaker as evidence.

azure: Responsible AI dashboard / Fairlearn on Azure ML

tradeoff: Adverse-impact monitoring is a batch aggregation with cohort thresholds; answer-quality uses LLM-as-judge — different tools for different signals.
Off-the-shelf fairness toolkits standardize the math; a custom harness fits the four-fifths + small-cohort caveats exactly.
HRIS/ATS APIs throttle, page, deliver duplicate webhooks, and allow manual edits — integrate defensively with idempotency and reconciliation.
A unified HR-API aggregator (e.g. Merge/Finch-style) cuts per-vendor work but adds a dependency and its own limits.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: The HRIS is the system of record, so the platform proposes/provisions into it rather than owning employee data.

say: Frame the gate as a legally consequential hiring decision plus fairness.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the agent auto-rejecting and list the legal and privacy failures.
Effective-dated policy grounding, then PII-scoped retrieval.
Humans decide adverse outcomes; the four-fifths adverse-impact monitor.
Compliance (LL144, EU AI Act) and fairness/quality eval.

expectations: Agent screens on skills and recommends.
Effective-dated policy grounding and PII-scoped retrieval.
Excludes proxies and instruments the four-fifths rule.
Designs identity-scoped PII retrieval and compliance evidence (LL144, EU AI Act).
Keeps humans deciding adverse outcomes; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you detect and exclude proxy features for protected traits?
How do you keep policy answers correct as policies change by date and jurisdiction?
What audit evidence does NYC LL144 require, and how do you produce it?
How does the fairness gate compare to the finance and legal verticals' gates?
A California leave policy changes at midnight but the vector index hasn't reindexed — what does an employee asking at 12:05 get, and how do you prevent a stale answer?
A recruiter rejects a candidate the agent recommended to advance — what is written to the audit, and how does it affect the adverse-impact metric?
An HRIS write times out after provisioning actually completed — how does the idempotent workflow avoid double-provisioning on retry?
A candidate asks for the AEDT notice and bias-audit summary — what do you return and from where?
A cohort is too small for a stable four-fifths metric — how do you report it without a misleading flag?
An employee asks a policy question that requires manager-only data — how does PII scope handle the refusal and escalation?
